TRANSCRIPT
Okay, this is Operation Quick Start, this is part 2 – the Mental Game. What I'm
talking about here, is what I like to think about as inner game versus outer game. And the
outer game's everything that happens outside yourself. And this is where most people
focus. It's like, what camera should I use? Where should I host my video? Where should I
host my website? What shopping cart should I use? Which microphone should I use?
What logo should I have? What merchant account should I use? Should that be an S Corp
or an LLC?
And those are all things that eventually you have to figure out, but they're not
nearly as important as your inner game. Your inner game is what happens in between
your ears. It's what's happening inside your body. It's way more important to shoot video
than to have the right camera. But pressing that record button and starting to talk, and
starting to speak, and create that video, that actually requires a lot more of your inner
game, of your mental game than figuring out what the camera is. You know, the reality is
you could use your phone and still create a super compelling, great video. But that
requires having your act together, mentally, internally. Having your inner game together
to have the confidence, to deliver, to have the confidence to start. Your inner game is way
more important than all those business details, because without your inner game you're
never gonna actually get started. You can think of infinite different things to think of in
that outer game world that you still have to figure out before you can get started.
If you decide that you have to get this perfect first, all this outer game stuff perfect,
there's no end to stuff you can invent that will stop you from actually making progress.
Sure, you'll figure out what camera to have. Now, what frame rate do you have? And what
settings? And what tripod? And what microphone? And what backdrop? No, the reality is
what you have to do is you have to push the record button and start speaking. That takes
the inner game. That's what's critical. That's what's going to allow you to start a business
and sustain a business, is having your inner game and getting your inner game fixed.
The reality is - business is about creation. That's what we do. At the end of the day,
we're creating. And everything that is created is created in the mind first, in the mind's
eye first. You have to see it first. You have to create it first internally before you created
out in the world. And you're going to have ups and downs. Your inner game, your mental
game is going to be up and down. There's times where you're gonna feel euphoric and
there's time you're gonna feel down to the dumps. Everyone has those ups and downs,
specially entrepreneurs. You have to expect that and you have to move through it. By the
way, I'm not immune to this. In twenty some years into this path, I'm still not immune to
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

Operation Quick Start Bonus Part 2 – Mental Game
© 2017 Internet Alchemy, Inc. 	I 	www.ProductLaunchFormula.com
it. And what I mean by immune to it, I mean immune to the depths. There's the highs and
the lows. And once you start your business, the highs get higher and the lows get lower.
That's just the reality. I've known hundreds, probably thousands of entrepreneurs and we
all have this, to some degree.
And you have to get your mental game in order, and you have to keep on working
on it. Keep on making it stronger. You have to expect big things of yourself. And equally
as important, you have to forgive yourself when you miss. This is probably the biggest
thing that I see among entrepreneurs, is they're really good at beating themselves up.
They do expect huge things and when they don't reach them, they get down in the dumps.
And then you become the self-perpetuating negative cycle of, "Wow, I didn't hit that huge
goal. Now I feel horrible so I'm going to go off and eat some ice cream instead of buckling
down and creating." You have to expect big things of yourself, and you have to forgive
yourself when you miss.
Successful people have a huge bias towards action. You don't need to be perfect, but
you need to be in motion. It's all about progress, not perfection. You can't expect to have
everything figured out before you start, you just have to get started. There's this saying
that I can't remember where it came from (Robert Frost), "Way leads on to way," which
means that as you get into motion, you'll see where you need to go in your next motion.
You'll see what your next step is. So don't expect to have everything figured out before you
start. You're going to make mistakes, we all do. We all have mistakes. You're going to have
false starts. You're going to start down one path and realize it's wrong. It happens to all of
us. We all have false starts. You just have to remember that if you don't decide, then
you've made a decision to stay in place, to stay where you are. So, it isn't about making the
perfect decision every time, it's about making a decision and then moving forward. The
plan is never complete. There's this saying I think it goes, "Man plans, God laughs."
The reality is, your plans will go awry, nothing will follow perfectly according to
plan. Dwight Eisenhower said, "The plan is nothing, planning is everything." You do have
to put together a plan, but you have to expect that the plan is not going to work perfectly.
General George Patton said, "A good plan violently executed today is better than a perfect
plan executed next week." A good plan violently executed today is better than a perfect
plan executed next week. Get into motion.
I started off with the Stock Newsletter. It was a newsletter about the stock market
that I sent initially once a month. And then after a couple of months, I started sending it
twice a month. That's how it started, with a simple email that I sent blind carbon copy. I
didn't have an email list; I didn't have an email provider. Anything like that. I just sent a
blind carbon copy, a newsletter, how to a bunch of friends and everyone I knew who had
an email address. I never thought I would literally change the business world. I was

desperate. I didn't know where I was going to go, but I just knew that if I started
publishing, then something good could come of it.
I like to think of golf. And I don't know if you play golf or not, but I've played a little
bit of golf in my life. It's not a huge thing in my life, but back when I was a teenager I
actually worked at a golf course and I got to play a bunch of golf. And what I noticed is
that, when you're golfing you have this little white ball and you want to hit it hundreds of
yards away, a quarter of a mile - or maybe even nearly a third of a mile away into a little
tiny hole. A three-and-a-half-inch hole.
So you're hitting this ball and you're just hoping that it's gonna end up in this hole
that's, let's say, nearly a third of a mile away. It's just sort of ludicrous to think of it, but
the thing is - I've watched people - beginners, absolutely beginners, on their very first
hole, and you give them 10 or 12, 13 shots, they're going to get that ball in a hole. And it
seems completely improbable, completely improbable. But I've seen this over and over
and how does it happen? It's because they get up and they swing at that ball and maybe
they miss the ball completely, maybe ... I've actually seen people succeed in hitting the
ball and then bounces backwards. Amazingly enough. It can happen. Or perfectly
sideways. So they're further away from the whole than they were before that shot. And
then they walk up and they take another swing at. And maybe this time it dribbles 30 feet
in the general direction of the whole.
But what they do is they gradually course correct until they get only a couple inches
away and they can tap the ball in the whole. And even absolutely beginners, like I said, 12,
maybe 13 or 14 shots and they can cover that third of a mile and get that ball in a whole,
because you course correct. You hit the ball in the generally in the correct direction and
even though it's not perfect, you course correct and you get closer and closer. And as you
get closer to the hole, the course corrections are smaller and smaller.
And this way it is in business. You take an action and then you assess. And then you
take another action and you assess. And you gradually move in the right direction, but
unless you take that action you can't course correct. You can't. And that's where it comes
back to the successful people having a huge bias towards action.
Now, one of the things you need to do, and this is big for your inner game, for your
mental game, is protect your confidence. There are going to be people that are dream-
stealers. You have to keep them out of your life. They will do it with good intentions,
because they love you and they care about you and they don't want to see you get burned.
They don't want to see you waste your time. They don't want to see you lose money. They
don't want to see you humiliated. So, they're going to tell you, "Don't do that, don't start a
business, don't try. Haven't you heard the statistics about people going out of business?"
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

Some of them will do with good intentions, but many people will do it out of jealousy or
envy. The reality is most people are not going to get what they want out of their life,
because they're not going to take action to create a better life.
And so, when you move into action, when they see you moving, it makes them face
that reality. And the reality is, they're just not putting in the effort to change their life.
And as long as they see you making that effort, it makes them feel worse and worse. And
so, they want to see you fail. They want to see you quit.
There's a saying, "Those who say it can't be done should get out of the way of those
who are doing it." I love that one, because a lot of people are going to tell you that you
can't do it. They are the dream-stealers. And you have to avoid those people. And
sometimes those people are close to you. They're close friends, they're parents, they're
children, they're siblings, they're aunts, uncles, they're people that are close to you.
Neighbors ...
And so you have to find a way to either not have those people on your life or don't
share your plans with them. You want to share your plans with people who will be your
champions. Not to everyone. Because there are a lot of people that will just rejoice in
seeing you fail. And try to find ways to make you fail.
Now, I know this can sound harsh, but this is all about protecting your confidence,
protecting your mental game so that you can move forward. So you have to be careful
about who you share your plans with. You want to listen to people with experience. Not
people who have no experience. If your brother in law’s never started a business before,
he's probably not the person that you want to listen to when he gives you advice about
starting your business.
I’ve had plenty of people tell me that my business is not going to work, that no one
makes money on the internet, that's impossible. That there's so many stock newsletters
out there. It's impossible. That there's so many people teaching about entrepreneurship.
It's impossible. Listen to people with experience. That have been down the path you're on
and that are going to be your champion. That are going to cheer you up. You need to find
your champions. You need to find the people that are going to cheer you on and those are
the people that you share with about what you're doing.
And you have to remember it's always about baby steps. This is something that
you'll hear a lot from me. I talk about baby steps a lot. And it's sort of like going back to
that golf analogy of hitting the ball in the right direction. Or sometimes you hit the ball in
the wrong direction, but then you just walk up and you take another shot. You take
gradual steps.

I mean, there's no baby who - the first time they try to walk - they just start
walking. They take one halting hesitant step and then they fall in their face. But then they
get up and try it again. And they fall down again. And they get up and try it again. And
then eventually they start taking small steps. And then, in not too long a time, they're
walking and then they're running. In business, you have to take baby steps. You just have
to take the next step.
Another piece that's a critical is celebrating your wins. And this is going back to
when I was talking about getting people to subscribe to my newsletter. Initially, it was ...
every few days I would get a subscriber. And that was just huge for me. And then, when I
got to getting one subscriber a day, that was huge. And then, three or four subscribers a
day and it was just amazing. And I wanted more, and I wanted more growth, but I
celebrated every time. I just had a meeting with my team and right now I've got this major
team. It's amazing how my business is growing.
But I've got this team, we sat down and we've looked at our wins over the last
couple of years. It was part of this exercise where we would look at our accomplishments.
And one of the accomplishment was we published a book. That's literally what someone
said, "Oh we've published a book." And then moved on to talk about the next
accomplishment, the next one, and I'm like, "Wait a second. We just didn't publish a
book. It was a number one New York Times best seller and it sold 100,000 copies." It's
just an example of where a lot of times people say, "Yeah, we did this, we did this." No,
you want to celebrate.
This doesn't mean you have to go out and have a big fancy dinner, but you have to
relish in the wins that you have. You have to recognize, because we get so busy in
business, it's so easy to forget, to recognize those wins.
And since I'm talking about wins, here's another ... as I thought about, what's the
difference between the successful people and the people that are not successful in
business? Successful people they just decide they're going to win. They know they're going
to win. They might not know how, but they know they're going to win. And they keep on
trying until they find something that works.
It's not like they put up one opt-in page and people aren't reacting to it, hardly
anyone subscribing, so they say, "I'm done. Bad business, doesn't work." They keep on
trying. They try a different offer. They try a different headline. They try a different way of
driving traffic. They just say, "This isn't working, I'm going to find something that does
work." And when they find something that does work, then they're like, "Okay, can I find
something that works better?" Unsuccessful people, they try something and if it doesn't
meet quick success, then they just quit. But successful people say, "Well, this isn't

working, I'm going to find something else that works." Just a matter of time.
Having a failure doesn't make you a failure. Just because something fails, doesn't
make you a failure. Failure is a thing, it's an event, it's not an identity. And as long as you
keep on moving forward and trying to iterate and come up with something, with a next
step, the next tweak, then you're not a failure. You're just a failure if you decide to quit. If
you decide you're done. If you decide it can't work for you. Successful people just keep
moving forward because they know they're going to win. It's just a matter of time until
they find a way they're going to win.
So this is all mental game and it's something I've spent a lot of time at. I've spent a
huge amount of time working on my inner game, my mental game, to make it as bullet
proof as possible. That's what will create a business for you. And that's what will sustain
you. There's going to be good times, there's going to be bad times in your business.
Everyone's got it. Your mental game is what's going to take you to the good times and
what's going to pull you back from the bad times.
When people first get involved in my work, they just want to know the tactics, the
tricks, that's just going to make money come flying into their business. But no money's
gonna come flying in, until you get your mental game in place. Until you get into action,
until you protect your confidence. Until you decide that you're going to win.
Now, in Part 2 (the Mental Game Part 2), I'm going to go on some of the strategies
and tactics I use to keep my mental game at high level. I've realized in this section, I was
pretty 50,000-feet high, I was giving the big picture. In part 2, I'm gonna go nitty gritty
and share some of the simple things I do every day and every week and every month to
keep my mental game at a very high level. So that's coming in the Mental Game Part 2,
but in the next section I want to talk about first steps to take within your business.
